terms.def: In-sample: data used to build the model. Out-of-sample (OOS): held-out data to test it. OOS performance is what matters.
Rolling train/test splits that advance through time; mimics live trading without look-ahead bias.
Using future data to make a past decision; the most common backtesting error — inflates backtest performance.
Only including stocks/funds that survived; inflates apparent returns because losers (delisted stocks) are excluded.
Return / risk metric used to evaluate strategy quality; deflated Sharpe accounts for multiple testing.
Bid-ask spread + market impact + commissions; realistic backtesting must include these or strategy looks better than it is.

eli3: Backtesting is testing a trading strategy on historical data to see how it would have performed in the past. If a strategy would have made money historically, it might be a valid strategy — or it might just have been lucky on that specific historical period. Backtesting is essential but dangerously easy to misuse.

A rigorous backtest simulates the real trading process: you only use data that would have been available at each point in time, you account for realistic transaction costs and market impact, and you measure out-of-sample (held-out) performance. Done properly, it's a powerful validation tool. Done carelessly, it's a data-mining exercise that will lose money in the real world.

The central challenge: with enough data and enough tries, any strategy will appear to have worked historically by pure chance. You need statistical discipline — proper hypothesis testing, out-of-sample validation, realistic cost assumptions — to distinguish genuine edge from overfitting.

whenWhy.title: The backtest pipeline: from signal to strategy
whenWhy.body: Complete backtest pipeline: (1) Universe definition: which assets, which time period, which corporate events (handle survivorship bias); (2) Signal construction: compute the signal at each time t using only data available at t (point-in-time); (3) Portfolio construction: convert signal scores to portfolio weights (ranking, optimisation, or simple long-short); (4) Execution simulation: apply realistic transaction costs (bid-ask, market impact, commissions); (5) Performance calculation: compute returns, risk metrics (Sharpe, drawdown, IR), factor attribution; (6) Significance testing: is the backtest Sharpe ratio statistically significant? (7) Validation: out-of-sample test, international replication, sub-period analysis. Each step is a potential source of bias — a bias at any step inflates apparent backtest performance.

whenWhy.title: Point-in-time data: the foundation of a valid backtest
whenWhy.body: Point-in-time (PIT) data uses only information that was actually available at each historical date — as if you were actually trading in real time. Why it matters: (1) Financial statements are restated — companies revise historical numbers; using restated data gives the strategy information it couldn't have had at the time; (2) Index membership changes retroactively — an S&P 500 backtest using today's constituents has survivorship bias (it includes only successful companies); (3) Earnings announcements: the release date matters — EPS data filed on date D is not available before D. Vendors: Compustat Point-in-Time database, S&P Capital IQ provide PIT data. Without PIT data, almost all fundamental backtests are contaminated by look-ahead bias. This single issue can inflate Sharpe ratios by 0.5-1.0 in fundamental factor strategies.

whenWhy.title: Measuring strategy performance: key metrics
whenWhy.body: Essential backtest metrics: (1) Annualised return: geometric mean annual return over the period; (2) Annualised Sharpe ratio: (return - risk-free) / σ; annualise daily: × √252; (3) Max drawdown: maximum peak-to-trough decline; critical for investor psychology and redemption risk; (4) Calmar ratio: annualised return / max drawdown; (5) Hit rate: fraction of profitable trades; (6) Profit factor: gross profit / gross loss; (7) Turnover: average annual portfolio turnover; determines transaction cost sensitivity; (8) Factor attribution: alpha after Fama-French five-factor regression; (9) Correlation to standard strategies (market, value, momentum); (10) Return in different regimes (bull/bear, high/low volatility). Red flags: very high Sharpe in backtest (>2.0) but no compelling economic reason; extremely low turnover that seems too good; Sharpe that disappears in subsamples or out-of-sample.

deepDiveIntro: Statistical significance of a backtest Sharpe ratio grows with the square root of the number of years: a Sharpe of 1.0 over 5 years gives a t-statistic of about 2.24, which is only marginally significant. The bar rises further when many strategies have been tried: if you tested 100 strategies and picked the best, you need a t-statistic of roughly 3.0 — not the usual 2.0 — to have convincing evidence of genuine skill. Lopez de Prado and Bailey (2014) formalized this multiple-testing adjustment into the Deflated Sharpe Ratio.

deepDive.heading: Walk-Forward Testing and Anchored vs. Rolling Windows
deepDive.body: Walk-forward testing divides history into sequential train/test chunks and re-calibrates the strategy on each training window before evaluating on the next held-out period. Anchored walk-forward grows the training window from a fixed start date; rolling walk-forward moves both ends forward to keep the training window a fixed size. You need at least 10 out-of-sample periods to judge whether performance is consistent or just the result of one lucky window. A strategy that looks great in one or two windows and poor in others is likely overfit to that specific period. [1]

deepDive.heading: Transaction Cost Modelling
deepDive.body: Underestimating transaction costs is the most common reason a backtest does not survive contact with live trading. The main cost components are bid-ask spread (wider for illiquid stocks), market impact from your own trades (larger orders move the price against you), broker commissions, and short-borrowing fees for short positions. A widely used rule of thumb for market impact is that it grows with the square root of your order size as a fraction of average daily volume. Always stress-test your strategy by doubling your cost assumption — a strong strategy should still be profitable. [2]

deepDive.heading: Combinatorial Cross-Validation and Deflated Sharpe Ratio
deepDive.body: The Deflated Sharpe Ratio adjusts the observed backtest Sharpe downward to account for how many strategies were tried before finding this one. If you tested 1,000 strategies, a lucky one would have a Sharpe around 1.5 by pure chance — so an observed Sharpe of 1.5 from 1,000 trials is essentially no evidence of skill. A DSR below zero means the result is not surprising given how much searching was done. Combinatorial purged cross-validation extends this by evaluating the strategy across all possible train/test splits rather than one, producing a full distribution of Sharpe estimates and a measure of how often the best in-sample parameters also win out-of-sample. [2]

takeaways: Point-in-time data is essential: look-ahead bias from restated financials inflates Sharpe by 0.5-1.0 in factor strategies. [1]
Walk-forward testing: re-calibrate on expanding training windows, test on held-out windows — 10+ periods needed. [1]
Transaction costs: model bid-ask, market impact (√(size/ADV)), borrowing; double costs in sensitivity analysis. [2]
Significance: t-stat = Sharpe × √T; with K strategies tested, require t-stat ≥ √(2 ln K) ≈ 3.0 for K=100. [2]
Deflated Sharpe Ratio (DSR): adjusts for multiple testing; DSR < 0 = no evidence of skill. [2]

edgeCases.q: How do you handle corporate actions and dividends in backtests?
edgeCases.a: Corporate actions require careful adjustment to avoid artificial return spikes: (1) Stock splits: adjust all pre-split prices by the split ratio; use adjusted price series (CRSP, Bloomberg adjusted close); (2) Dividends: total return index includes reinvested dividends; price-only index doesn't. For a momentum backtest: use total returns (dividend-adjusted) — a stock with high dividend yield looks like it dropped in price without dividend adjustment, which distorts the momentum signal; (3) Mergers/acquisitions: when company A acquires company B, remove B from the universe at announcement (not deal close) to avoid holding a takeover target at the premium price; (4) Spin-offs: create new entities; handle the new entity as a new stock entry; (5) Delistings: handle carefully — companies that went bankrupt (price → 0) must be included to avoid survivorship bias. CRSP handles all these adjustments; Compustat requires more careful processing.

edgeCases.q: What is the minimum backtest length for a given strategy?
edgeCases.a: The minimum backtest length depends on: (1) The strategy's Sharpe ratio (higher Sharpe = shorter history needed for statistical significance); (2) The number of strategies tested (more testing = longer history needed); (3) The signal frequency (daily signals need fewer years than monthly signals for the same number of independent observations). Rule of thumb for minimum meaningful backtest: Sharpe 0.5 strategy: need 10+ years for 2-sigma significance; Sharpe 1.0: need 5 years. With 100 strategies tested: these requirements increase by factor √(2 ln 100)/2 ≈ 1.5×. For high-frequency strategies: use the number of independent trades (need 100+) rather than calendar length. For factor strategies: 20+ years ideal; allows testing across multiple full economic cycles (at least 2 full recessions). Strategies with < 5 years of history or < 3 full economic cycles should be treated as unvalidated.

sources.label: Lopez de Prado, M. — Advances in Financial Machine Learning, Wiley
Bailey, D.H. & Lopez de Prado, M. (2014) — The Deflated Sharpe Ratio, JPM
